# Questions in Livestream 115

Live Stream #115: The Magic of a Good Question

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:17</a> | Why are some scholars trying to read Genesis 1:1 more concordistically? | He argues the push comes from pressure to harmonize Genesis with modern science and says the grammatical case for the newer reading is not yet persuasive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:26</a> | Did Dr. Falk study under Donald Redford? | He says no, though he did study under scholars in Redford's orbit and comments on Redford's reputation and influence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=1063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:43</a> | How reliable are Plutarch and Diodorus for Egyptian myth? | He treats them as late and often unreliable for very ancient material, useful only with caution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=1474" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:34</a> | Why are there "Cushite" references in Northwest Mesopotamia in Judges 3:10, and was Moses' wife one of these people? | He argues the issue likely involves reading Kishite rather than Cushite and ties the problem to vocalization and later pointing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=1699" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:19</a> | In the Cushite/Kishite issue, does Dr. Falk mean matres lectionis or later niqqud? | He clarifies that he means matres lectionis-style vowel letters from the Iron Age, not later Masoretic niqqud. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=1962" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:42</a> | What is the funniest interaction you have ever had with a fundamentalist? | He says he has not had many funny interactions with fundamentalists and instead gives examples of odd interactions with Ron Wyatt supporters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=2364" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:24</a> | Do Amarna letters 74 and 290 mention Bit-Ninurta near Jerusalem, and could it be read as the Temple of Solomon? | He says he found the relevant wording in letter 290, trusts Rainey's rendering, and rejects the proposed Solomon reading as etymologically untenable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=2775" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:15</a> | Were abstract Egyptian gods such as Heka and Ma'at considered deities by Moses' time or only concepts? | He says they were still regarded as deities in Moses' time, with the concepts associated with gods rather than merely abstract ideas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=2931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:51</a> | What were the Exodus plagues, and were they all attacks on Egyptian gods? | He reads them as extreme but natural hydrological and agricultural events and says the Bible explicitly frames only the final plague as judgment on the gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=3268" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:28</a> | Do Deuteronomy's warfare laws fit an Egyptian setting from roughly 1500 to 1000 BC? | He generally agrees that the laws make good sense in that context, though he wants to see the argument's specific reasoning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=3453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:33</a> | What do churches need more of today in preaching or education? | He says pastors and congregations need to return to serious biblical study, expository preaching, and biblical literacy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=4804" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:04</a> | Is there any possibility that Deuteronomy 6:4 was tainted by identifying Yahweh/Jehovah as Elohim? | He rejects the idea and argues that later claimed revelation must be consistent with the Old and New Testaments rather than correcting them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=4999" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:19</a> | If Kent Griffith is confident in his scholarship, should he be challenged to present at the University of Toronto or the University of Chicago? | He says such revisionist arguments would not get that kind of academic audience and that debating them usually becomes a contest over whether evidence exists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=5252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:32</a> | If Abraham is an Amorite, could "wandering Aramean" in Deuteronomy 26:5 be a geographical identifier rather than an anachronism? | He says the verse is about Jacob and treats the phrase as both a retrojection and a geographic identifier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=5495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:35</a> | What is the current state of minimalist versus maximalist debates in biblical archaeology beyond sites such as Jericho and Hazor? | He says the debate has moved toward a broad middle, with major questions now focused more on scope, chronology, Moses, the Exodus, and the extent of the Davidic and Solomonic kingdoms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=5756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:56</a> | Who is Dr. Falk's favorite Egyptologist? | He names Kenneth Kitchen, citing Kitchen's scholarship, stories, and his own time as Kitchen's research assistant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=5852" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:32</a> | Where does the seventh-century Egyptian context for the Exodus come from? | He ties it partly to Jack Holladay's excavation argument about Tell el-Maskhuta/Pithom and says later evidence from Tell el-Retaba undermines that late-date inference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=6082" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:22</a> | What is the best explanation for the death of the mummy of Ramesses II? | He says Ramesses II most likely died of old age and rejects drowning claims based on the mummy evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=6293" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:53</a> | How much did Dr. Falk know about the Bible before getting his first Bible-related degree? | He says he already knew a fair amount, including Hebrew and apologetics, but seminary greatly expanded his knowledge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=6952" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:52</a> | What was Hur's relationship to Moses? | He says Scripture does not clearly give a marital or blood relationship, though Hur was an important Judahite leader and Bezalel's grandfather. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=7352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:32</a> | Would Dr. Falk team up with David Rohl musically? | He says no, because he has different musical tastes and prefers to control composition, orchestration, and production himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=7967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:47</a> | Could God having "reconsumed" the Ark of the Covenant hint that its final destiny was planned, whether destroyed or lost? | He says Jeremiah gives the best testimony, suggesting the temple articles went to Babylon and that the ark was probably destroyed there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=8256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:36</a> | What might have happened if the Babylonians had tried to destroy the ark in some other way? | He says destruction by fire would not be sacrilege, but deliberately profaning it could have brought divine judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=8347" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:07</a> | Is it possible Nebuchadnezzar's statue was made with gold from the ark? | He says it is possible, since gold purified by fire would no longer be tied to the ark's sacred function. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=8423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:23</a> | Was most of the ark's body just gold-plated acacia wood? | He says yes, but the cladding was likely thick gold plate rather than thin gold leaf. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=8569" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:49</a> | Could ancient workers hammer gold cladding very thin? | He says they could make thin foil, but ritual furniture often used thicker cladding because it had to hold tooling and sometimes be removed intact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=9093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:33</a> | Why do early kings, prophets, and judges not have many Yahwistic names while many Yahwists have El and Baal names? | He says names are a weak basis for reconstructing Deuteronomistic history and that the evidence and methodology are debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=9417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:57</a> | What makes someone considered a scholar? | He says scholarship usually involves a body of scholarly work, sound methodology, and often a terminal degree, though degrees alone do not make someone a scholar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=10157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:17</a> | Is James White's scholarly work good? | He says he cannot really judge it as scholarly work because he sees White's output as popular rather than peer-reviewed scholarly contribution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=10396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:16</a> | Which revisionist theories that Dr. Falk disagrees with does he wish were true? | He jokingly says he wishes American young-earth creationist chronology were true because living dinosaurs would be exciting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=10562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:02</a> | What should viewers make of Kathleen Kenyon-style claims that there was no conquest at Jericho? | He argues the issue is more complicated than a simple denial and points to late Bronze evidence and later work that challenge the standard reduction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=10876" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:16</a> | Would an Ancient Near Eastern scholar need to know the difference between Hittites and Amorites to be considered good? | He says yes, especially if the scholar claims expertise in the conquest period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=11190" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:30</a> | What is Dr. Falk's favorite food from Egypt? | He first jokes about McDonald's, then praises an apple-banana chicken curry from a restaurant on Luxor's West Bank. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=11568" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:48</a> | What does camel taste like? | He says well-prepared camel tastes like a milder beef with a pot-roast-like texture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=11797" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:37</a> | What other science fiction is Dr. Falk deeply into besides Dune? | He mentions classic Doctor Who, Star Wars, Babylon 5, The Expanse, The X-Files, and related series. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=12323" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:23</a> | What is something interesting about Revelation, its Old Testament connections, prophecy, or how to read it? | He says Revelation should be read as apocalyptic wisdom literature whose symbolism is decoded through the Old Testament. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=12815" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:35</a> | Has Dr. Falk worked with Muslim Egyptologists, and what does he think of their perspectives? | He says he has, distinguishes Egypt-trained and foreign-trained scholars, and says their training affects whether they stress tourism, monuments, preservation, or academic method. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/63Yt1EIc8Ng?t=13168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:28</a> | Why would Muslim Egyptologists be hostile toward Genesis or Exodus if those texts could validate the Quran? | He says many view Genesis and Exodus as corrupted revelation and that modern political tensions also shape suspicion toward biblical connections. |
